entries.description: SRA (AAAI 2025) applies attention-rationale alignment using a single fixed head (layer 8, head 7) without principled head selection. IoU-F1 = 0.539 on HateXplain test set (4.5× over vanilla BERT).
entries.notes: Direct concurrent work. Single-head limitation is our key differentiation point.

entries.description: SRA uses softmax attention with MSE alignment loss applied only to hate/offensive examples with non-empty rationale masks. No sparsemax operator used anywhere in the architecture.
entries.notes: Gap: sparsemax + rationale supervision never combined.

entries.description: SMRA (2026) extends SRA with moral rationale supervision (6 Moral Foundation dimensions). Achieves +7.4 pp IoU-F1 on Portuguese dataset. Same softmax-only architecture as SRA.
entries.notes: Moral rationale approach is Portuguese-centric; same mechanistic limitations as SRA.

entries.description: HateXplain annotates 20,148 social media posts with 3 labels: hate/offensive/normal. Rationales are token-level spans from 3 independent annotators. ~31% of test examples may have annotation inconsistencies (per SRA).
entries.notes: Primary benchmark. Variable annotator agreement is both a challenge and an experimental opportunity.

entries.description: Jain & Wallace (2019): Attention weights are frequently uncorrelated with gradient-based feature importance; different attention distributions can produce identical predictions.
entries.notes: Motivates supervised attention — passive attention is not faithful, but supervised attention may be.

entries.description: BERT attention heads specialize: syntactic/positional/delimiter heads identified by Clark et al. Voita et al. show ~80% prunable. Michel et al. provide gradient-importance scoring formula I(h,l) = E_x[|∂L/∂A^{CLS,h,l}|].
entries.notes: Head importance scoring formula directly applicable to our head selection step.

entries.description: Sparsemax achieves comparable NMT/NLI performance to softmax while producing sparse (many exact-zero) attention weights. Sparsemax loss is a natural alignment loss for sparse binary rationale targets.
entries.notes: Core technical foundation for replacing softmax in supervised heads.

entries.description: Adaptively sparse transformers (α-entmax): per-head learned sparsity in NMT. Heads naturally learn different α preferences. Improves head diversity and interpretability vs. softmax.
entries.notes: Motivates that head-specific sparsity control is feasible and beneficial.

entries.description: ERASER standardizes comprehensiveness and sufficiency as faithfulness metrics (faithfulness = model uses its explanation for prediction). IoU-F1 and Token-F1 as plausibility metrics (explanation matches human rationale).
entries.notes: Our evaluation protocol follows ERASER definitions directly.

entries.description: D3CODE (EMNLP 2024): Annotator moral values explain offensiveness disagreement more strongly than demographic factors (geography, age, gender). Cross-cultural agreement is higher for explicit hate (slurs, threats) than subtle discrimination.

Integrated Gradients satisfies Sensitivity (relevant features get non-zero attribution) and Implementation Invariance (two equivalent models get identical attributions). Only requires forward+backward passes with no model modification.
entries.notes: IG is the correct replacement for LIME in our faithfulness evaluation pipeline.

entries.description: No paper in the literature applies sparsemax as the attention activation operator simultaneously with attention-rationale supervision in hate speech or any NLU task with human annotations.
entries.notes: Primary novelty gap. Confirmed by reviewing SRA, SMRA, MRP, and general sparsemax/entmax literature.

entries.description: No paper combines gradient-based head importance scoring (Michel et al. formula) with rationale supervision to perform principled head selection before applying alignment loss.
entries.notes: SRA uses fixed head 7 layer 8 with no justification. Head selection is unexplored in supervised attention context.

entries.description: SRA and all prior work use ≤3 training seeds. For effect sizes observed (IoU-F1 improvements ~0.1-0.2), statistical power at n=3 is approximately 30-40%. None report bootstrap confidence intervals.
entries.notes: Statistical rigor gap: 5 seeds + bootstrap CIs required for credible NeurIPS submission.

entries.description: LIME used as primary faithfulness evaluator in HateXplain, SRA, and SMRA. LIME stability on short texts (~20 tokens) has not been validated for this domain. IG-attention agreement never computed for hate speech.
entries.notes: Methodological gap: LIME oracle assumption is questionable for short social media posts.
